name: Bromine
name_origin: From the Greek 'bromos' meaning 'stench', a reference to the element's sharp, irritating odor; named by its discoverer Antoine-Jerome Balard in 1826.

occurrence.abundance_summary: Bromine is a moderately rare element with a crustal abundance of roughly 2.4 ppm. It is concentrated in seawater (~65 ppm) and especially in inland brines such as those of the Dead Sea and underground deposits in Arkansas, USA; major producers include the United States, Israel, China, and Jordan.
states: bromide ion (Br-) in seawater and underground brines

compounds.name: Hydrogen bromide / hydrobromic acid
compounds.summary: Strong mineral acid used in the manufacture of inorganic bromides and as a reagent in organic synthesis.

compounds.name: Silver bromide
compounds.summary: Pale-yellow solid used historically as the principal light-sensitive compound in photographic film and paper.

compounds.name: Sodium bromide
compounds.summary: Soluble salt used in oilfield drilling fluids, as a sedative (formerly), and as a swimming-pool sanitizer precursor.

compounds.name: Methyl bromide
compounds.summary: Colorless gas formerly used as an agricultural soil fumigant; phased out under the Montreal Protocol due to its ozone-depleting potential.

compounds.name: Brominated flame retardants
compounds.summary: Family of compounds (e.g., decabromodiphenyl ether, TBBPA) added to plastics, textiles, and electronics to inhibit combustion.

uses: Brominated flame retardants in plastics and electronics
Drilling fluids (dense calcium and zinc bromide brines) for oil and gas wells
Pharmaceuticals and agrochemicals (synthesis of brominated intermediates)
Mercury-emission control sorbents in coal-fired power plants
reactivity_summary: Bromine is a dense, deep red-brown liquid that readily evaporates to give a corrosive, suffocating vapor. It is the only nonmetal that is liquid at room temperature, alongside mercury among elements as a whole. Bromine is a strong oxidizer, less reactive than chlorine but more reactive than iodine; it reacts vigorously with most metals to form bromides, adds across alkene double bonds, and disproportionates in alkaline solution to bromide and bromate.
isotopes_summary: Bromine has two stable isotopes in nearly equal abundance: 79Br (~50.69%) and 81Br (~49.31%). About 30 radioisotopes are known; the longest-lived is 77Br (half-life ~57.0 hours). 82Br (half-life ~35.3 hours) is used as a tracer in hydrology and industrial process studies.

citations.source: CRC Handbook of Chemistry and Physics, 102nd ed.
Royal Society of Chemistry - Periodic Table: Bromine
